data.title: BP rises as rivals said to weigh possible takeover - FT
data.description:
BP (NYSE:BP) +3.1% pre-market Friday following a Financial Times report that several rival oil producers have "run the numbers" in weighing the opportunity to buy the struggling company at a steep discount.

Shell (SHEL), Chevron (CVX), Exxon Mobil (XOM), TotalEnergies (TTE) and Abu Dhabi's Adnoc have been analyzing a potential deal, while oil trader Vitol could be interested in elements of the business, according to the report.

Takeover talk started earlier this week from a Bloomberg report that Shell (SHEL) was studying the possibility of a BP (NYSE:BP) purchase.

Ongoing underperformance by BP (BP) shares - the stock has dropped 27% over 12 months - "makes it open to a takeover," a source close to activist investor Elliott Management, which holds a stake of just over 5% in the company, told FT.

BP's (BP) oil and gas assets alone are worth $82B, according to UBS analyst Joshua Stone - above the current $75B market value - but the company is weighed down by $77Bn of debt and long-term liabilities, including those stemming from the 2010 Deepwater Horizon oil spill.

data.title: 1-800-Flowers.com shares wilt on bleak Q1 results; CEO steps down
data.description:
Shares of 1-800-Flowers.com (NASDAQ:FLWS) are limping through Friday’s premarket trading and expected to open at the lowest level in more than a decade as gloomy first quarter results drove shares more than 30% lower ahead of the open.

“While we are deeply disappointed by the quarterly results, we are steadfast in our commitment to turning this underperformance around," said Jim McCann the current CEO of 1-800-Flowers.com.

Reflecting on the disappointing results and need for innovative ideas and new leadership for the company, CEO McCann made the decision to step down from the company he founded nearly five decades ago, passing the torch to former Home Depot (HD) executive Adolfo Villagomez.

“In this case, the best thing I can do is fire myself and hire someone who’s much more skilled than I am in the areas that are important to the company going forward,” McCann said in an interview with The Wall Street Journal.

During the critical holiday season, issues tied to order fulfillment with Harry & David products, inaccurate orders, and damaged products reportedly cost the company as much as $20M in lost sales and refunds, reverberating through the first quarter in diminished demand and resulting 12.6% decline in revenue to $331.5M, $33M less than Wall Street expected. This contributed to an adjusted loss of $0.71 per share, significantly worse than a loss of $0.28 per share in the same quarter last year and below expectations by $0.37. Adjusted EBITDA loss widened to $34.9M, versus a loss of $18.0M last year.

The $4.6M cost to replace and refund orders during the Q4 technical snafu, coupled with a highly promotional sales environment, weighed on the company’s gross profit margin, which declined by 350 basis points to 33.1%.

By category, sales in the company’s Consumer Floral & Gifts segment were down 11.4% and fell 18.2% for Gourmet Foods and Gift Baskets. BloomNet, a network of florists used to fulfill orders and offer various business solutions to florists, realized a 4.5% improvement in sales.

The latest quarterly results, along with the “rapidly evolving macroeconomic landscape and uncertainties that continue to shape the near-term outlook,” led 1-800-Flowers.com (NASDAQ:FLWS) to withdraw its near-term guidance.

data.title: Federal Reserve may be put in 'difficult' position from higher tariffs
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Michael Barr expects that tariffs could put the Federal Open Market Committee in a "difficult position" if both inflation and unemployment increase, he said in a speech on Friday.

He expects the higher level of tariffs, which are "without modern precedent," will lead to higher inflation in the U.S. and lower growth in the U.S. and abroad starting later this year. However, it's too early to say how tariffs will affect the economy, because the final form of the tariff policy isn't yet known.

"In my view, higher tariffs could lead to disruption to global supply chains and create persistent upward pressure on inflation," he said.

"I am equally concerned that tariffs will lead to higher unemployment as the economy slows. Thus, the FOMC may be in a difficult position if we were to see both rising inflation and rising unemployment."

Still, the U.S. economy started Q2 in a "relatively strong position," with unemployment low and the disinflationary process gradually progressing. "Given the economy's strong starting point and the progress we have made in bringing inflation back toward our 2% objective, monetary policy is in a good position to adjust as conditions unfold,"  Barr said.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: N2OFF via Solterra expands European footprint with entry into fourth project; shares rise
data.description:
• N2OFF (NASDAQ:NITO) Friday announced Solterra’s entry into the Polish renewable energy market by participating in the financing of a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) project in Poland.
  • The project, currently planned at 35MW/140MWh, represents a significant step in expanding Solterra’s large-scale energy storage solutions in the region, the company said.
  • N2OFF, along with other lenders, will assist with financing an initial payment under a signed grid connection agreement.
  • Future development plans include applying for an increase in capacity to more than 100MW/400MWh.
  • N2OFF (NASDAQ:NITO) shares up 36% to $0.52 in premarket trading.

data.title: Italy's Moltiply sues Google in €2.97B lawsuit over alleged market dominance
data.description:
Update: The story was updated with a statement from Google.

Italy's Moltiply Group said its unit was suing Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG) Google and is seeking damages of €2.97B (about $3.34B) for alleged abuse of its dominant market position as previously recognized by the EU's Court of Justice.

Moltiply said on Friday that its subsidiary 7Pixel, which operates the price comparison service Trovaprezzi.it, recently notified several "Google Group entities of a follow-on claim for damages for the abuse of dominance that favored Google Shopping between 2010 and 2017, as finally determined by the European Court of Justice in September 2024."

Moltiply noted that the amount of damages estimated by the experts commissioned by 7Pixel, including structural effects of the abuse and interest, is equal to €2.97B.

Google noted that it is reviewing the claim.

"The changes Google made in 2017 following the European Commission’s decision are working as intended and the number of comparison shopping sites in Europe using our shopping features has multiplied from just 7 to more than 1,550. We disagree strongly with these exorbitant private damages claims which disregard this successful and growing industry," said a Google spokesperson in an emailed statement to Seeking Alpha.

The claim was filed at a Milan court, Reuters reported, citing Italian daily Corriere della Sera, which did not give further details.

Last year, in September, the EU's top court upheld a fine of €2.42B imposed on Google for abusing its dominant position by favoring its comparison shopping service, Google Shopping, in search results. In 2017, the Commission accused Google of favoring Google Shopping, thereby disadvantaging competitors.
